paper_title: Advances in paleoclimate data synthesis and analysis of associated uncertainty: towards data–model integration to understand the climate

methods.description: This method describes the process of collecting individual paleoclimate time series from various archives and proxies, retrieving associated metadata, harmonizing the data and metadata into standardized formats, and creating synthesized products like regional stacks, spatial maps, or comprehensive databases for specific archives or variables. This process facilitates data-model integration and comparison.

steps.description: The starting point of the data-model integration process is formed by individual paleoclimate time series. Over the past decades thousands of paleoclimate time series based on different proxy sensors and derived from different geological archives have been generated.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and locate individual paleoclimate time series data from various archives and proxies.
inputs: Information about available paleoclimate archives and proxies
outputs: List of potential paleoclimate time series sources

steps.description: Meaningful use of paleoclimate time series thus requires laborious retrieval and some form of harmonisation of data and metadata. This process can be facilitated through the use of dedicated software, such as PaleoDataView (Langner and Mulitza, 2019), which is specifically designed for standardisation and visualisation of time series from marine sediment archives.
steps.searchable_summary: Retrieve paleoclimate time series data and associated metadata from identified sources.

steps.description: Meaningful use of paleoclimate time series thus requires laborious retrieval and some form of harmonisation of data and metadata. This process can be facilitated through the use of dedicated software, such as PaleoDataView (Langner and Mulitza, 2019), which is specifically designed for standardisation and visualisation of time series from marine sediment archives. These contributions show how the value of the individual time series is increased through harmonisation of ontologies, metadata and chronological information.
steps.searchable_summary: Harmonize paleoclimate data and metadata, including standardizing formats, ontologies, and chronological information.

steps.description: Further contributions make use of such synthesis efforts to provide temporally or spatially continuous coverage that can be compared more easily with simulations or used as boundary conditions for climate model experiments. Thus, the pollen database (Cao et al., 2020) has been used to derive maps of land cover (Cao et al., 2019), and Paul et al. (2021) transformed previously synthesised data (MARGO Project Members, 2009) to provide a global map of sea surface temperature including uncertainty for the last glacial maximum. The gridding method used to construct the global sea surface temperature map also holds promise to extrapolate paleoclimate observations that have generally coarse spatial distribution. Two contributions show how individual time series can be used to derive continuous regional paleoclimate time series. Building on previous work (Lisiecki and Stern, 2016), Peterson and Lisiecki (2018) provide regional stacks of benthic foraminifera stable carbon isotopes... Fuhrmann et al. (2020) present continental aridity time series spanning 60 000 years based on multiple proxies from key regions.
steps.searchable_summary: Create synthesized paleoclimate data products from harmonized data, such as spatial maps, regional stacks, or comprehensive databases.
outputs: Synthesized paleoclimate data products (e.g., spatial maps, regional stacks, databases)

steps.description: All data underlying the syntheses as well as the derived data products are publicly available (see individual papers for access).
steps.searchable_summary: Make synthesized paleoclimate data and derived products publicly available.

methods.description: This method describes various approaches used to quantify and analyze different sources of uncertainty in paleoclimate proxy records, including signal-to-noise ratio, timescale-dependent uncertainty, noise from taxonomic composition, and uncertainty in temporal representativeness. This quantification is crucial for meaningful interpretation and data-model integration.

steps.description: For meaningful interpretation of paleoclimate data, it is crucial to be able to separate signal from noise. This requires first a quantification of the uncertainty associated with the indirect recording of climate by proxies.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and define sources of uncertainty inherent in paleoclimate proxy data.
inputs: Understanding of proxy systems and their limitations
outputs: Defined sources of uncertainty relevant to the specific proxy data

steps.description: Two contributions to this special issue aim to quantify the signal-to-noise ratio of proxy time series directly by assessing the correlation among nearby paleoclimate time series that should have experienced the same climate (Reschke et al., 2019; Münch and Laepple, 2018). Both studies indicate a timescale-dependent signal-to-noise ratio in paleoclimate time series. This timescale dependence of proxy uncertainty is explored further in two companion papers (Kunz et al., 2020; Dolman et al., 2021). In part one, Kunz et al. (2020) develop a theoretical framework for a spectral approach to estimate timescale-dependent uncertainty, and this concept is applied in part two (Dolman et al., 2021). The studies above focussed on geochemical proxy sensors, whereas Jonkers and Kučera (2019) present an approach to detect noise in reconstructions based on the taxonomic composition of microfossil assemblages. A final contribution to the set of papers exploring paleoclimate data uncertainty provides a first attempt to quantify the error due to uncertainty in temporal representativeness (Amrhein, 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: Apply specific methods to quantify different types of paleoclimate data uncertainty, such as signal-to-noise ratio, timescale-dependent uncertainty (e.g., spectral analysis), noise from taxonomic composition, or temporal representativeness error.
inputs: Paleoclimate time series data (potentially multiple nearby series)
outputs: Quantified uncertainty metrics (e.g., signal-to-noise ratio, timescale-dependent uncertainty estimates, noise estimates, temporal representativeness error)

steps.description: Together these papers provide important new constraints on the uncertainty of paleoclimate data. They present new ways to detect and quantify various aspects of the uncertainty and thus present new tools for the paleoclimate (data) community. The spectral approach comes together with opensource code (Dolman et al., 2021) to facilitate broad use.
steps.searchable_summary: Utilize quantified uncertainty information for interpreting paleoclimate data and integrating it with climate models.
outputs: Improved paleoclimate data interpretation considering uncertainty
